turns.content: friend i have question about water temperature for shrimp. rainy season now and temperature drop at night, you know. my shrimp not eating much
That's a common issue during rainy season! When water temperature drops below about 26-27°C, shrimp appetite decreases significantly. How low is the water temperature getting at night in your ponds?
i check this morning at 4am, maybe 24 degree. sometime 23. big pond worse because more open area, you know. wind come across and cool everything down

Yeah, 23-24°C is definitely on the low side for optimal shrimp feeding. At that temperature, their metabolism slows down quite a bit. A few things you can do:

1. **Reduce feed** temporarily — don't keep feeding the same amount when they're not eating, because uneaten feed just rots and tanks your water quality. Cut to maybe 60-70% of normal.
2. **Feed during the warmest part of the day** — shift feeding to late morning and early afternoon when the water's warmed up a bit.
3. **Check your dissolved oxygen** — cooler water holds more oxygen which is good, but decomposing uneaten feed eats it up fast.

How big is your pond? And is it the same issue across all of them or mostly the big one?
i have 3 pond total. big one is 1.2 hectare, that one the problem. two small one, each about 0.6 hectare, they not so bad because more shelter from tree around, you know

That makes sense — the 1.2 hectare pond has a lot more surface area exposed to wind and rain, so it loses heat faster. The smaller 0.6 hectare ponds being sheltered by trees really helps them retain warmth.

For the big pond, a couple of options:

- **Windbreaks** — even temporary ones like netting or bamboo screens on the side where the wind comes from can reduce heat loss a lot.
- **Deeper water** — if you can raise the water level a bit, the extra depth acts as thermal mass. Deep water stays warmer at the bottom.
- **Reduce aeration at night slightly** — aerators can accelerate cooling by mixing surface water. But be careful not to cut too much or you'll lose oxygen.

Are you managing all three ponds yourself? That's a lot of ground to cover, especially at 4am checks.
i hire 2 worker for heavy job. carry feed bag, fix net, dig when need. but checking pond, checking shrimp, that i do myself. every morning. nobody know my pond like me, you know

I respect that — you can't delegate the things that require that deep knowledge of your own ponds. A farmer who checks his own stock every morning is going to catch problems early.

So for the big pond right now, I'd say the priority is reducing feed waste. At 23-24°C, try cutting the feed by about 30-40% and concentrate the feeding between 10am and 2pm when the water's at its warmest. Watch the feeding trays — if there's still leftover after 2 hours, cut back more.

The windbreak idea is a longer-term fix but even a row of banana plants or bamboo on the north side would make a difference for next rainy season.

What species are you raising? Vannamei or monodon?
vannamei. white shrimp. everybody here in mekong grow vannamei now, you know. monodon too slow, price not much better

Good choice — vannamei are definitely the workhorse of the industry. They're more tolerant of density and grow faster, which makes the economics work better.

The good news is vannamei can handle down to about 22°C for short periods without major die-off. So 23-24°C is stressful but not dangerous. The main risk is secondary — poor appetite leads to uneaten feed, which causes ammonia and nitrite spikes, and that's what actually kills shrimp.

So the key actions right now:
1. Cut feed by 30-40%, feed only midday
2. Monitor water quality closely — check ammonia and pH especially
3. Keep an eye on the feeding tray in the big pond
4. If you have aerators, run them more during the day (to warm and oxygenate) and less at night

Once the rainy season passes and temperatures come back up, you can ramp the feed back up. How long does rainy season usually last in your area?

maybe until end of november, then dry up. but this year rain more than normal. my 2 worker say they never see this much rain, and they work for me long time already. 1.2 hectare pond water level go up 15 centimeter in one week

That's a significant water level rise — 15cm in a week means a lot of fresh rainwater diluting your pond. That's actually another problem on top of the temperature: the salinity drops when that much fresh water comes in. Vannamei can tolerate low salinity but sudden drops stress them out.

A few things to watch:

- **Salinity** — if it's dropping below 5 ppt, that's getting into risky territory for vannamei. Check it daily if you can.
- **Drainage** — do you have overflow gates on the big pond? You might want to let some of the surface water drain off, since the rain sits on top and is the coldest, freshest water.
- **Minerals** — heavy rain dilutes the mineral content. If you have access to calcium/dolomite, a light application can help stabilize the water chemistry.

The fact your workers are noticing unusual rain is worth paying attention to. Sometimes these extra-heavy seasons bring problems that normal routines can't handle. Are your pond banks holding up okay with all that water?

bank okay for now. i build good bank, you know. thick. but i worry about big pond. i go check gate tomorrow morning. maybe open little bit, let water go out slow. salinity i check yesterday, about 8 ppt. not too bad but if more rain come...

8 ppt is okay for now — vannamei are comfortable at 5-35 ppt, so you have some buffer. But you're right to be concerned because if it keeps raining and drops below 5, you'll see more stress and molting problems.

Opening the gate slowly is the right call. Let the top layer drain off — that's where the freshest, coldest water sits. Don't drain too fast or you'll stress the shrimp with the sudden change. Maybe 3-5cm per day is a safe rate.

Also, after you drain some off, if you have access to seawater or salt, you can top up a bit to stabilize the salinity. Some farmers in the Mekong keep a reserve of salt for exactly this situation.

Your instincts are good — check the gate, drain slowly, keep monitoring. Sounds like you've been doing this a long time.
long time yes. but weather now not like before, you know. every year different. thank you friend. i go check gate early morning. maybe 4am like always

You're welcome! You're right, the weather patterns are shifting and every season brings new surprises. But 3 ponds and still out there at 4am — that's dedication.

Quick summary for tomorrow:
- Open the gate on the big pond just a little, drain the top layer slowly
- Cut the feed by 30-40%, only feed midday
- Check salinity again, especially on the big pond
- Watch the feeding trays for leftovers

Good luck with the rainy season. Come back anytime if you need help figuring things out.
